items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Japanese woodblock prints influenced European modern art
items.transcript: Japanese woodblock prints, known as ukiyo-e, transformed European art in the late nineteenth century in ways that still surprise people. So here's the situation. When Japan opened up to trade in the 1850s, these colorful prints started arriving in Paris, sometimes as wrapping paper around imported goods. Artists there had never seen anything quite like them. Now, what made ukiyo-e so different? Think about a traditional European painting: careful shading, realistic depth, a single fixed viewpoint. Ukiyo-e prints ignored all of that. They used flat areas of bold color, strong outlines, and daring, off-center compositions. A subject might be cropped at the edge, as if glimpsed by accident. It turns out this was exactly what restless young painters were looking for. Take Vincent van Gogh. He collected hundreds of these prints and even copied several directly, studying how they arranged space. Edgar Degas borrowed their unusual angles, placing figures at the very edges of his canvases. And the Impressionists admired how a print could suggest a whole scene with just a few simple shapes. You can see the lesson they absorbed: a painting doesn't have to imitate reality exactly to feel alive. It can flatten, simplify, and still move you. That shift, away from strict realism toward bold design, helped open the door to modern art. So these small prints, once used as mere packaging, quietly reshaped how European artists thought about what a picture could be.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Japanese woodblock prints influenced European modern art
options.B: How Vincent van Gogh built a large print collection
options.C: How Japan opened its ports to European trade
options.D: Why European painters preferred lifelike realism and detail

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 概括主旨：日本浮世绘版画如何影响欧洲现代艺术。B 只聚焦梵高收藏和临摹版画这一个细节，属 too_narrow。C 谈日本开港通商，只是背景，并非讲座主题，属 wrong_topic。D 用了讲座里的「European、realism」等关键词，却是描述传统欧洲绘画的写实而非主旨，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that the prints arrived as wrapping paper?

options.A: To explain how woodblock prints were manufactured in Japan
options.B: To show these prints arrived almost by accident
options.C: To describe what the imported goods were wrapped in
options.D: To argue that Europeans valued Japanese goods over art

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 说明该句意图：强调这些有影响力的版画最初几乎是偶然传入欧洲的。A 讲版画如何制作，与该句意图无关，属 wrong_purpose。C 只是复述「包裹进口货物」这一字面内容，没解释为什么提它，属 literal_restatement。D 把它拔高成「欧洲人更看重日货而非艺术」，是教授未表达的过度推断，属 over_inference。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did ukiyo-e prints differ from traditional European painting?

options.A: They relied on precise shading for lifelike depth
options.B: They portrayed historical battles in muted earth tones
options.C: They used flat, bold color and off-center compositions
options.D: They merged many separate viewpoints into one unified scene

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与讲座一致：浮世绘用平涂的鲜明色彩和偏离中心的构图。A 说靠精细明暗表现立体感，那恰恰是欧洲传统绘画的特点，方向反了，属 reversed_logic。B 说描绘历史战役、用大地色，讲座从未提及，属 topic_adjacent。D 说把多个视角合并成一个画面，讲座说的是偏心构图而非合并视角，属 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that a subject might be cropped at the edge?

options.A: To explain how the prints were first used as packaging
options.B: To state that some figures appear near the edges
options.C: To claim European artists disliked centered compositions
options.D: To illustrate the daring off-center framing that inspired painters

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 说明该句意图：举例展示那种大胆的偏心构图，正是它启发了欧洲画家。A 讲版画当包装纸，与该句意图无关，属 wrong_purpose。B 只是把「人物出现在边缘」这一现象复述一遍，没解释目的，属 literal_restatement。C 断言欧洲艺术家讨厌居中构图，是教授未表达的过度推断，属 over_inference。

items.subject: psychology
items.transcript: Confirmation bias is one of the most powerful and least noticed habits of the human mind. Here's what it means: we tend to search for, favor, and remember information that confirms what we already believe, while quietly ignoring evidence that contradicts it. That habit is called confirmation bias. Now, this isn't the same as lying to ourselves on purpose. It happens automatically, often without our awareness. Let me give you a classic study. In the 1960s, psychologist Peter Wason gave people a sequence of three numbers and asked them to figure out the hidden rule. Most participants formed an early guess and then only tested examples that fit that guess. They rarely tried cases that might prove them wrong. Because they kept seeking confirmation, many never discovered the actual rule. Think about how this plays out in daily life. When you already hold an opinion, say, about a political issue, you'll click on headlines that agree with you and scroll past the ones that don't. Sound familiar? Over time, your view feels more and more certain, even if you haven't really examined the other side. That's confirmation bias quietly shaping what you notice. It affects scientists, doctors, investors, all of us. The good news is that awareness helps. Researchers have found that deliberately asking what evidence would prove me wrong can loosen the bias's grip. Next time, I want to look at a related tendency, one that's even trickier. It's about how, once we learn an outcome, we convince ourselves we knew it all along.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Peter Wason first uncovered a hidden numeric pattern
options.B: How confirmation bias shapes the information we notice
options.C: How strong political opinions form during public debates
options.D: Why human memory fails to store new evidence

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 概括主旨：确认偏误如何塑造我们所注意到的信息。A 只聚焦 Wason 的数字实验这一细节，属 too_narrow。C 谈政治观点如何形成，是相关但不同的话题，属 wrong_topic。D 用了「memory、evidence」等词却曲解成记忆存储失败，讲座并未这样说，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did most participants do in Wason's study?

options.A: They guessed the hidden rule correctly on the first try
options.B: They refused to state any guess about the rule
options.C: They tested only examples that fit their early guess
options.D: They mostly tried cases designed to disprove their own guess

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与讲座一致：多数参与者只测试符合自己早期猜测的例子。A 说第一次就猜对规则，讲座说很多人始终没发现真正规则，属 wrong_detail。B 说他们拒绝给出任何猜测，讲座说他们都很早形成了猜测，属 topic_adjacent。D 说他们主要尝试反驳自己猜测的情况，恰恰相反，方向反了，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does research suggest can reduce confirmation bias?

options.A: Memorizing even more facts that already support their beliefs
options.B: Avoiding all political news and social media
options.C: Trusting their first instinct about an issue
options.D: Deliberately asking what evidence would prove them wrong

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 与讲座一致：研究发现刻意问「什么证据能证明我错」可以松动偏误。A 说去记更多支持已有信念的事实，那只会加重偏误，方向反了，属 reversed_logic。B 说彻底不看政治新闻和社交媒体，讲座没这么建议，属 topic_adjacent。C 说相信第一直觉，讲座说这恰恰会强化偏误，属 wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: A tendency to assume we knew the outcome
options.B: New statistical methods for analyzing survey data
options.C: More details of Wason's original number experiment
options.D: How people gradually forget the outcomes they once predicted

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 对应结尾预告：一种「事后觉得自己早就知道结果」的倾向（后见之明偏误）。B 谈统计方法，讲座结尾没预告这个，属 wrong_topic。C 只是回到本讲已讲过的 Wason 实验细节，而非「下一个」话题，属 too_narrow。D 说人们逐渐忘记曾预测的结果，与预告「我们说服自己早就知道」正好相反，属 reversed_logic。

items.subject: music
items.topic: the role of improvisation in early jazz
items.transcript: Improvisation sits at the very heart of early jazz, and it's what set the music apart from almost everything that came before it. So what do we actually mean by improvisation? It's the art of inventing music in the moment, composing and performing at the same time, rather than reading every note from a written score. In the classical tradition, a performer usually plays exactly what the composer wrote. Early jazz musicians did something bolder. Now, picture a small band in New Orleans around 1915. The players would take a familiar melody, maybe a popular song or a blues tune, and use it only as a starting point. From there, each musician would spin new variations, bending the notes, adding runs, playing with the rhythm. Take, for instance, a cornet player like Louis Armstrong, and you'll hear him reshape a melody so freely that it becomes almost a brand-new tune. And here's the key: much of this happened collectively, an approach called collective improvisation. Several players improvised at once, weaving their lines together, responding to each other in real time. This is why no two performances of the same song ever sounded quite alike. Each one was a unique event, gone the moment it was played. That freedom made jazz feel alive and personal, but it also depended on deep shared knowledge, a common musical language every player understood. Next time, we'll trace how this improvised style spread north to Chicago and began to change.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Louis Armstrong freely reshaped melodies on the cornet
options.B: How jazz eventually reached cities beyond New Orleans
options.C: How improvisation defined and shaped early jazz music
options.D: Why classical performers follow a written musical score

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 概括主旨：即兴演奏如何定义并塑造了早期爵士乐。A 只聚焦阿姆斯特朗改造旋律这一个例子，属 too_narrow。B 谈爵士乐后来的传播，是下一讲的预告而非本讲主题，属 wrong_topic。D 用了「written musical score」等词却在讲古典演奏，是误导性使用关键词，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did early jazz musicians use a familiar melody?

options.A: They played it exactly as the composer wrote it
options.B: They wrote it down carefully before each concert
options.C: They avoided popular songs and blues tunes entirely
options.D: They used it only as a starting point

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 与讲座一致：他们把熟悉的旋律只当作出发点，再加以变化。A 说完全照作曲家所写演奏，那是古典传统而非爵士，属 wrong_detail。B 说每场演出前仔细写下来，与即兴恰恰相反，属 topic_adjacent。C 说他们完全回避流行歌和布鲁斯，讲座恰恰说他们以这些为素材，方向反了，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did no two performances of the same song sound exactly alike?

options.A: Each performance was newly improvised in the moment
options.B: Each band used a completely different written score
options.C: Each city had its own distinct jazz style
options.D: Each musician memorized the whole melody note for note

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 与讲座一致：每次演出都是当场即兴，所以独一无二。B 说各乐队用完全不同的乐谱，讲座强调的是即兴而非乐谱，属 wrong_detail。C 说每个城市有自己的风格，讲座没提城市风格差异，属 topic_adjacent。D 说每位乐手把旋律逐音背下来，与即兴恰恰相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: How early classical music first influenced the jazz style
options.B: How the improvised style spread north to Chicago
options.C: More about Louis Armstrong's cornet playing technique
options.D: Why jazz musicians abandoned improvisation over time

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 对应结尾预告：这种即兴风格如何北传到芝加哥并开始变化。A 谈古典音乐对爵士的影响，讲座结尾没预告这个，属 wrong_topic。C 只是回到本讲已讲的阿姆斯特朗吹奏细节，而非下一个话题，属 too_narrow。D 说乐手后来放弃即兴，与预告「风格传播并发展」正好相反，属 reversed_logic。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: how stalactites and stalagmites record environmental changes
items.transcript: Deep inside a limestone cave, hanging from the ceiling and rising from the floor, are formations that quietly keep a record of the ancient climate. These are stalactites and stalagmites, known as speleothems. So how do they form, and how can a lump of rock possibly store climate history? Let me explain. Rainwater seeps down through soil and rock, dissolving tiny amounts of calcium carbonate along the way. When a drop finally emerges inside the cave, it leaves behind a trace of mineral. Over thousands of years, drop by drop, these deposits actually grow into the icicle-like shapes you see in photographs. Here's the remarkable part. As a speleothem grows, it adds thin layers, a bit like the rings of a tree. Each layer locks in a chemical signature from the water that formed it. Scientists carefully slice a stalagmite open and measure the ratio of certain oxygen isotopes in each band. Now, that ratio depends on temperature and rainfall at the time the layer formed. So by reading the layers from bottom to top, researchers can reconstruct how climate shifted over tens of thousands of years. Think about what that means. A single stalagmite can preserve a detailed weather diary from long before humans kept any records. And because caves are sheltered, that archive stays remarkably undisturbed. This is why speleothems have become one of the most valuable tools we have for understanding the deep history of Earth's climate.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How rain slowly dissolves minerals deep underground
options.B: How limestone caves are slowly carved out over time
options.C: How tree rings reveal the age of forests
options.D: How cave formations record Earth's past climate changes

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 概括主旨：洞穴沉积物如何记录地球过去的气候变化。A 只聚焦雨水溶解碳酸钙这一步骤，属 too_narrow。B 谈石灰岩洞如何被侵蚀形成，讲座讲的是洞内沉积物而非洞本身，属 wrong_topic。C 用了「tree rings」这个类比词却当成主旨，实际那只是打比方，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do speleothems grow?

options.A: Dripping water leaves mineral traces that build up
options.B: Wind carries sand that slowly hardens on the ceiling
options.C: Molten rock cools and crystallizes inside the cave
options.D: Water dissolves the formations layer by layer

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 与讲座一致：滴水留下矿物痕迹，日积月累堆积成形。B 说风带来沙子在洞顶硬化，讲座说的是滴水沉积，属 wrong_detail。C 说岩浆冷却结晶，那是火成岩的形成方式，与本讲无关，属 topic_adjacent。D 说水把沉积物层层溶解，与「堆积生长」恰恰相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare a speleothem's layers to the rings of a tree?

options.A: To point out that limestone caves protect their contents
options.B: To show each layer records one period of time
options.C: To state that speleothems are shaped like icicles
options.D: To prove that trees and caves form in identical ways

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 说明该类比的意图：每一层都像树的年轮一样记录了一段时间。A 讲洞穴为何隐蔽，与该类比意图无关，属 wrong_purpose。C 只是复述「石笋形状像冰锥」这一字面内容，没解释类比目的，属 literal_restatement。D 断言树和洞穴以完全相同的方式形成，是教授未表达的过度推断，属 over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about speleothems as a source of climate data?

options.A: They have completely replaced all other methods of studying climate
options.B: They record climate only from the most recent centuries
options.C: They preserve climate records from before written human history
options.D: They form fastest in caves near the ocean

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与教授暗示一致：石笋能保存人类有文字记录之前的气候信息。A 说它已完全取代其它所有气候研究方法，教授只说它很有价值，属 over_generalization。B 说它只记录最近几个世纪，教授说的是数万年的跨度，方向反了，属 reversed_logic。D 说它在近海洞穴长得最快，讲座未支持，属 topic_adjacent。
